claims.claim_text: The opening healthcare SaaS breach-and-audit-loss anecdote is presented as a real incident with specific dates, business impact, and remediation timing.
claims.anchor_text: In September 2023, a healthcare SaaS company running on GKE discovered that their network policies were not being enforced. A penetration tester demonstrated that a compromised pod in the `staging` namespace could freely communicate with pods in the `production` namespace, despite NetworkPolicy resources that should have blocked cross-namespace traffic. The root cause: the cluster was using the legacy iptables-based kube-proxy dataplane, which does not enforce Kubernetes NetworkPolicy at all. The team had assumed that creating NetworkPolicy resources was sufficient---they did not realize that enforcement requires a CNI that supports it. The compliance violation cost them a SOC 2 audit failure, delaying a $2.3 million enterprise deal by four months. The fix took 30 minutes: enable Dataplane V2 on their next cluster creation. The business impact lasted a quarter.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams sometimes discover too late that creating `NetworkPolicy` objects is not enough on its own. Enforcement depends on the cluster's networking implementation, so you should verify that your GKE cluster is using a dataplane and policy engine that actually enforce the rules you define.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war story with named timing and quantitative business impact, and part of the technical explanation is also overstated.

claims.claim_text: Creating a Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resource has no effect unless the cluster uses a networking solution that supports NetworkPolicy enforcement.
claims.rationale: This is a Kubernetes API behavior documented in the upstream NetworkPolicy reference.

claims.claim_text: VPC-native is the default network mode for new GKE clusters created with the CLI or API on version 1.21.0-gke.1500 and later.
claims.span_hint: section: VPC-Native Clusters and Alias IPs
claims.rationale: The GKE alias IPs documentation explicitly states the default network mode by version and creation method.

claims.claim_text: In a VPC-native cluster, Pods get alias IPs from secondary ranges and those Pod IPs are natively routable within the VPC.
claims.rationale: The GKE VPC-native cluster documentation describes alias IP allocation and Pod routability inside the VPC.

claims.claim_text: By default, Standard GKE assigns a /24 Pod CIDR per node for up to 110 Pods, so IP planning can constrain cluster size.
claims.rationale: Current GKE documentation gives the default Pod-per-node value and the corresponding /24 node CIDR allocation.

claims.claim_text: The size of the Pod secondary range must be planned at cluster creation because it is immutable after creation.
claims.rationale: GKE documents the Pod secondary range size as an immutable cluster-creation-time decision.

claims.claim_text: GKE Dataplane V2 is implemented with eBPF and Cilium and is enabled by default for new Autopilot clusters.
claims.rationale: The Dataplane V2 concept page states the implementation details and default Autopilot behavior.

claims.claim_text: With Dataplane V2, Kubernetes NetworkPolicy enforcement is built in and does not require a third-party add-on such as Calico.
claims.span_hint: section: Network Policies with Dataplane V2
claims.rationale: GKE documents built-in enforcement for Dataplane V2 clusters and contrasts it with add-on management.

claims.claim_text: Network policy logging on GKE is available only on Dataplane V2 and can log allowed and denied connections.
claims.rationale: The network policy logging guide explicitly documents availability and the allowed-versus-denied logging controls.

claims.claim_text: The Datadog logging anecdote is presented as a real operational incident with a specific denied-connection rate.
claims.anchor_text: **War Story**: A platform team enabled network policy logging and discovered that their monitoring agent (Datadog) was making 3,000 denied connections per minute to pods in restricted namespaces. The agent had broad scrape targets configured, and every denied connection generated a log entry. Before enabling logging in production, test in a staging environment to understand the log volume---it can be surprisingly high.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **War Story**: Network policy logging can generate much more data than teams expect, especially when broad agents or probes repeatedly hit denied paths. Before enabling logging in production, test in a staging environment and review the projected log volume.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident-style anecdote with a named product and precise per-minute number.

claims.claim_text: A GKE Ingress resource configures a Google Cloud Application Load Balancer for HTTP(S) traffic.
claims.rationale: The GKE Ingress documentation states that creating an Ingress causes GKE to create and configure the corresponding Application Load Balancer.

claims.claim_text: A GKE Service of type LoadBalancer creates an external passthrough Network Load Balancer by default.
claims.rationale: The GKE LoadBalancer Service documentation describes the default external passthrough Network Load Balancer behavior.

claims.claim_text: GKE supports Gateway API and recommends transitioning toward Gateway and Route resources for their increased capabilities.
claims.span_hint: section: Gateway API: The Future of Kubernetes Routing
claims.rationale: The GKE Gateway API concept page compares Ingress and Gateway and recommends transitioning over time.

claims.claim_text: GKE provides GatewayClasses such as gke-l7-global-external-managed, gke-l7-regional-external-managed, gke-l7-rilb, and gke-l7-gxlb with specific underlying load balancer mappings.
claims.rationale: The GKE Gateway API documentation includes a GatewayClass table mapping these classes to their load balancer implementations.

claims.claim_text: HTTPRoute supports weighted backendRefs for traffic splitting in GKE Gateway traffic management.
claims.span_hint: section: Canary Deployments with Gateway API
claims.rationale: GKE traffic management documentation explicitly describes weighted traffic splitting with HTTPRoute backendRefs.

claims.claim_text: HTTPRoute can match requests using HTTP headers.
claims.rationale: The GKE Gateway API concept page states that HTTPRoutes can match based on request-header details.

claims.claim_text: Private Service Connect gives the GKE control plane a private endpoint in your VPC and keeps node-to-control-plane traffic private.
claims.span_hint: section: Private Service Connect for GKE
claims.rationale: The PSC concept page describes the internal control-plane endpoint and private communication model.

claims.claim_text: When a private GKE control plane has no public endpoint, clients can reach it only from networks with private connectivity and authorized access.
claims.rationale: Google Cloud's private-pool tutorial states that private-endpoint control planes are reachable only from instances with VPC access and authorized-network access.

claims.claim_text: The Meta/eBPF and 26% latency benchmark item combines unsupported vendor-specific scale claims with a precise benchmark figure.
claims.anchor_text: **Dataplane V2 uses the same eBPF technology that powers Meta's (Facebook's) entire network stack.** Meta processes over 600 billion eBPF events per day across their fleet. In GKE, Dataplane V2's eBPF programs are compiled and loaded into the Linux kernel at node boot, where they intercept and process packets before they ever reach userspace. This is why Dataplane V2 can achieve 26% lower latency than iptables-based routing in benchmarks with 10,000+ services.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Dataplane V2 uses eBPF to move key routing and policy decisions into the kernel.** In practice, this helps reduce some of the rule-chain bottlenecks and observability gaps of legacy iptables-based networking.
claims.rationale: This combines uncited third-party operational numbers with a precise benchmark claim that is not backed by an allowlisted primary source here.

claims.claim_text: The large-cluster item gives an incorrect Pod limit and mixes several precise scale numbers into one unsupported fact cluster.
claims.anchor_text: **A single GKE cluster can support up to 65,000 nodes and 400,000 pods.** The practical networking limit is usually IP exhaustion rather than cluster capacity. A /14 pod CIDR gives you roughly 262,144 pod IPs. If each node uses a /24 for pods (the default for 110 max pods per node), you can support about 1,024 nodes before running out of pod IPs. Planning your IP ranges at cluster creation is one of the few decisions you truly cannot change later.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **GKE can run very large clusters, but in practice Pod IP planning often becomes an earlier limit than the headline cluster-size ceiling.** Planning your IP ranges at cluster creation is one of the few networking decisions that is hard to change later.
claims.rationale: The Pod-limit figure is wrong against current GKE limits, and the item bundles multiple precise numbers that should not remain uncited.

claims.claim_text: The Gateway API working-group item overstates contributor specifics while the role-separation lesson is the real durable point.
claims.anchor_text: **The Gateway API was designed by a cross-vendor working group** including engineers from Google, Red Hat, HashiCorp, and VMware. The key insight was that Ingress combined infrastructure concerns (TLS, IP addresses) with application concerns (routing rules) in a single resource, making it impossible to safely delegate to different teams. Gateway API's three-tier model (GatewayClass, Gateway, HTTPRoute) maps directly to the cluster admin, platform team, and application team roles that exist in most organizations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **The Gateway API was designed as a role-oriented Kubernetes API.** One of its core ideas is to separate infrastructure concerns from application routing through resources such as GatewayClass, Gateway, and HTTPRoute.
claims.rationale: The vendor roster is unnecessarily specific for the teaching goal, while the standards-level design lesson can be preserved without the uncited contributor list.

claims.claim_text: The Maglev item incorrectly implies that GKE's Global Application Load Balancer uses Maglev and adds uncited historical performance specifics.
claims.anchor_text: **GKE's Global Application Load Balancer uses Google's Maglev system**, which was published as a research paper in 2016. Maglev is a distributed software load balancer that runs on commodity servers at Google's edge PoPs. It uses consistent hashing to achieve connection persistence without shared state between load balancer instances. A single Maglev machine can handle 10 million packets per second, and the system has been running Google's production traffic since 2008.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Google Cloud load balancing is built on several underlying systems, and the exact technology depends on the load balancer type rather than a single implementation for every product.**
claims.rationale: Current Google Cloud documentation maps Application Load Balancers to GFE/Envoy-based technologies, not Maglev, so the item is materially inaccurate as written.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes row says policies are ignored without Dataplane V2, which is too broad and misleading.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes | row: Assuming NetworkPolicy works without Dataplane V2
claims.anchor_text: | Assuming NetworkPolicy works without Dataplane V2 | Creating policies without enforcement | Enable Dataplane V2 at cluster creation; without it, policies are ignored |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Assuming NetworkPolicy works automatically | Creating policies without verifying enforcement | Ensure the cluster has a network-policy-capable dataplane or add-on configured; creating `NetworkPolicy` objects alone does not guarantee enforcement |
claims.rationale: Legacy GKE can enforce NetworkPolicy with supported configurations, so the blanket claim that policies are ignored without Dataplane V2 is inaccurate.

claims.claim_text: A deny-all egress NetworkPolicy also blocks DNS unless you explicitly allow traffic to kube-dns/CoreDNS on UDP and TCP port 53.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes | row: Forgetting DNS egress in NetworkPolicy
claims.rationale: This follows directly from upstream NetworkPolicy behavior for egress isolation and DNS-dependent name resolution.

claims.claim_text: Private nodes in a private GKE cluster have no external IPs and typically need Cloud NAT or equivalent private access for outbound pulls.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes | row: Not enabling Cloud NAT for private clusters
claims.rationale: Google Cloud's private-cluster guidance states that private nodes lack external IPs and points to Cloud NAT for outbound internet access.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes row says you should choose only one of Ingress or Gateway API per cluster, which is broader than current GKE guidance.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes | row: Mixing GKE Ingress and Gateway API on the same cluster
claims.anchor_text: | Mixing GKE Ingress and Gateway API on the same cluster | Both create load balancer resources | Choose one approach per cluster; Gateway API is the recommended path forward |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Mixing GKE Ingress and Gateway API on the same cluster | Both controllers can provision load balancer resources | Avoid exposing the same application path through both during normal operation; use a clear migration plan if you run both |
claims.rationale: Current GKE docs say Ingress and Gateway can be used simultaneously, so the cluster-wide prohibition is overstated.

further_reading.title: VPC-native clusters
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative GKE reference for alias IPs, Pod routability, and network-mode defaults.

further_reading.title: GKE Dataplane V2
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains how Dataplane V2 works, its Cilium/eBPF basis, and its operational limits.

further_reading.title: About Gateway API
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers GKE GatewayClasses, the Gateway/HTTPRoute model, and GKE's migration guidance from Ingress.

further_reading.title: About Private Service Connect
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains PSC-based control plane access and the private-endpoint networking model for modern GKE clusters.

notes: Quiz answers and hands-on exercise steps were excluded per instructions. Code-block comments were also excluded from the seed; separately, the comment claiming that 64 max Pods per node yields a /26 appears inconsistent with current GKE Pod-CIDR sizing docs and should be reviewed outside this citation pass.
